does: This paper introduces Proto-RM, a reward modeling framework that applies prototypical networks to RLHF to improve data efficiency. The core contribution is leveraging metric learning and prototype-based classification to learn stable reward representations from limited human preference annotations. The method enables reward models to generalize better with fewer training examples by organizing the learned representation space around prototypical examples of preferred and dispreferred responses. Experiments across multiple datasets show that Proto-RM achieves comparable or superior performance to standard reward models while requiring substantially less human feedback, demonstrating meaningful gains in both reward model accuracy and downstream LLM performance on preference-sensitive tasks.
connects: This work builds on the established pipeline of RLHF for LLM alignment, which has become standard practice following early applications to instruction-tuning. It directly addresses a known bottleneck in RLHF: the annotation cost and scalability challenges of collecting human preference judgments at scale. The approach combines two research directions: prototypical networks from few-shot learning literature, which have shown effectiveness in learning from limited labeled data through metric learning, and recent work on data-efficient fine-tuning of language models. The paper responds to growing interest in sample-efficient reward modeling and extends prior work on improving RLHF scalability through techniques like offline RL and active learning.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more practical deployment of RLHF in resource-constrained settings, enabling preference-based alignment of LLMs with reduced annotation budgets. The prototypical network approach to reward modeling could generalize to multi-task or domain-specific alignment scenarios where collecting comprehensive human feedback is prohibitive. Future work could explore combining Proto-RM with active learning strategies to identify which examples most benefit from human annotation, or extending the framework to contrastive learning objectives. The efficiency gains may enable broader application of RLHF to specialized domains and non-English languages, and could inform design of reward models for increasingly capable language models where alignment becomes correspondingly more important.
